rough_idea: A system that integrates multiple testing tools and LLMs for automated unit testing in an enterprise environment.
user_stories:
As an experienced stakeholder representative, I understand that a system like this must serve multiple, often conflicting, needs—from the developer who needs speed to the administrator who needs compliance. The user stories must reflect the entire lifecycle of automated testing, from code commit to deployment.

Here is a comprehensive set of user stories, categorized by user type and function, covering functional and non-functional requirements.

---

## 🧪 I. Developer User Stories (The Code Writer)

These stories focus on the developer's immediate workflow: writing, testing, and iterating on code quickly.

| User Story | Type | Context & Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **As a Developer, I want the system to automatically generate initial unit test skeletons based on the function signature and existing code logic, so that I can start writing tests immediately without boilerplate setup.** | Functional (LLM) | *Justification:* Developers hate boilerplate. This feature leverages the LLM's understanding of code structure to provide a starting point, significantly reducing the time spent on setup and increasing focus on test logic. |
| **As a Developer, I want to submit a pull request (PR) and have the system run a comprehensive suite of unit tests against the new changes, so that I receive immediate feedback on potential regressions before merging.** | Functional (Integration) | *Justification:* This is the core CI/CD loop. The feedback must be instant and highly visible within the PR interface (e.g., GitHub/GitLab comments). |
| **As a Developer, I want the system to highlight specific lines of code that are poorly covered by existing tests (low coverage areas), so that I know exactly where to focus my testing efforts.** | Functional (Reporting) | *Justification:* Simple coverage reports are insufficient. Developers need actionable insights—the system must pinpoint the *why* and *where* of the gaps. |
| **As a Developer, I want to easily adjust the complexity or scope of the LLM-generated tests (e.g., "make this test more robust," or "focus only on edge cases"), so that I can guide the AI and ensure the tests meet specific business requirements.** | Functional (Control) | *Justification:* Developers must maintain control. The LLM is a tool, not a replacement. Providing fine-grained control over the prompt/generation process is critical for trust and accuracy. |

## 🛡️ II. QA Engineer/Tester User Stories (The Quality Gatekeeper)

These stories focus on the QA professional's need for governance, review, and deep testing coverage.

| User Story | Type | Context & Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **As a QA Engineer, I want to define custom, complex test scenarios (e.g., "test failure when input is null AND user role is 'Guest'"), so that I can ensure the system handles specific, high-risk business edge cases that the LLM might overlook.** | Functional (Input) | *Justification:* LLMs are excellent at general patterns but fail at highly specific, domain-specific business rules. QA must be able to inject this critical, human-defined knowledge. |
| **As a QA Engineer, I want the system to provide a visual dashboard showing test coverage trends over time (e.g., "Coverage dropped by 5% in the last sprint"), so that I can track the overall quality health of the codebase and justify refactoring efforts.** | Functional (Reporting/NFR) | *Justification:* Quality is a trend, not a single metric. The dashboard must provide historical context to demonstrate technical debt and guide architectural decisions. |
| **As a QA Engineer, I want the ability to manually review, approve, and modify any test generated by the LLM, so that I maintain ownership of the test suite's quality and accuracy.** | Functional (Workflow) | *Justification:* This is a mandatory human-in-the-loop requirement. The system must facilitate review, not bypass it. The QA engineer must be the final arbiter of quality. |
| **As a QA Engineer, I want the system to categorize and tag generated tests (e.g., `@Security`, `@Performance`, `@EdgeCase`), so that I can run targeted subsets of tests for specific release gates.** | Functional (Organization) | *Justification:* Running the entire suite for every minor change is slow. QA needs the ability to run highly focused, risk-based test subsets (e.g., only security tests before a release). |

## ⚙️ III. DevOps/CI/CD Operator User Stories (The Integrator)

These stories focus on reliability, speed, and seamless integration into the enterprise pipeline.

| User Story | Type | Context & Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **As a DevOps Engineer, I want to configure the system to automatically trigger a full unit test run upon every successful merge to the `main` branch, so that we never deploy untested code.** | Functional (Integration/Trigger) | *Justification:* This defines the "gate." The system must be the final, non-bypassable gatekeeper before deployment. |
| **As a DevOps Engineer, I want the system to support multiple, isolated execution environments (e.g., Python 3.8, Java 11, Node 16), so that we can test legacy codebases alongside modern services.** | Functional (Compatibility) | *Justification:* Enterprise environments are rarely monolithic. The system must be polyglot and support diverse runtime environments. |
| **As a DevOps Engineer, I want the system to provide detailed logs for failed tests, including the full stack trace, environment variables, and the exact code version that failed, so that debugging is efficient and reproducible.** | Functional (Logging/Debugging) | *Justification:* When a test fails in a CI pipeline, the logs must be exhaustive. Simply knowing "it failed" is useless; the *why* is paramount. |
| **As a DevOps Engineer, I want the system to cache test results and dependencies between runs, so that subsequent test executions are significantly faster and do not waste compute resources.** | Non-Functional (Performance) | *Justification:* Speed is critical in CI/CD. Caching results and dependencies is essential for maintaining developer velocity and reducing pipeline runtime. |

## 🧑‍💻 IV. System Administrator User Stories (The Governance Manager)

These stories focus on security, compliance, and system maintenance.

| User Story | Type | Context & Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **As a System Administrator, I want to manage and restrict access to specific testing tools and LLM APIs (e.g., only the Security team can use the advanced vulnerability scanner), so that we adhere to the principle of least privilege.** | Functional (Security/Access Control) | *Justification:* In an enterprise setting, access must be granular. Admins must control who can use powerful, potentially costly, or sensitive tools. |
| **As a System Administrator, I want to audit every test run, including who triggered it, which code version was tested, and the specific LLM prompts used, so that we maintain a clear compliance trail for regulatory requirements.** | Non-Functional (Compliance/Audit) | *Justification:* This is non-negotiable in regulated industries (finance, healthcare). Every action, especially those involving AI generation, must be traceable for compliance audits. |
| **As a System Administrator, I want to configure rate limits and cost tracking for external LLM API calls, so that we prevent unexpected billing spikes and manage cloud expenditure.** | Non-Functional (Cost/
qa_pairs:
Q1: ### Question 1: What specific criteria should the system use to determine "poorly covered" lines of code? **Rationale:** This question is crucial because it directly impacts the functionality and effectiveness of the developer's ability to focus on critical areas. Clarifying these criteria will ensure that the system can accurately identify and highlight lines of code that are under-tested, thereby guiding developers more effectively.  ### Question 2: How should the system handle edge cases where the LLM-generated tests do not fully cover business-specific requirements? **Rationale:** This question is essential because it addresses a critical gap in the developer's control over the AI-generated tests. Understanding how the system will manage these scenarios ensures that developers can maintain their trust and confidence in the tool, while also ensuring compliance with specific business rules.  ### Question 3: What are the specific types of logs and environment details required for failed test runs? **Rationale:** This question is important because it directly impacts the DevOps Engineer's ability to debug issues efficiently. By specifying what information needs to be included in the logs, we can ensure that the system provides sufficient detail to facilitate quick resolution of failures.  ### Question 4: How will the system handle conflicts between custom test scenarios defined by QA Engineers and LLM-generated tests? **Rationale:** This question is critical because it addresses potential integration issues between human-defined test cases and AI-generated ones. Understanding how these conflicts are resolved ensures that the system maintains a balance between automation and manual oversight, preventing any unintended behavior.  ### Question 5: What specific security measures will be in place to protect sensitive data during LLM API calls? **Rationale:** This question is essential for ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements and maintaining user trust. By specifying the security measures, we can ensure that sensitive data is handled appropriately, reducing the risk of breaches or misuse.  ### Prioritization: 1. **Question 1**: This directly impacts developer productivity and the effectiveness of test generation. 2. **Question 2**: Ensures that developers maintain control over AI-generated tests while adhering to business-specific requirements. 3. **Question 3**: Facilitates efficient debugging by providing necessary information for DevOps Engineers. 4. **Question 4**: Prevents conflicts between custom and AI-generated tests, ensuring a smooth integration process. 5. **Question 5**: Ensures compliance with security standards and protects sensitive data.  These questions will significantly impact the system design and implementation by addressing key functional and non-functional requirements, ensuring that all stakeholders' needs are met effectively.
A1: 1. Branch coverage under 50%. 2. Developers can manually override them. 3. Full stack trace and exact env vars. 4. Custom tests always take precedence. 5. PII redaction before sending to LLM API.
req_draft:
### Functional Requirements (FR)

1. **FR-001: Automated Test Skeleton Generation**
   - **Title:** The system automatically generates initial unit test skeletons based on the function signature and existing code logic.
   - **Description:** When a developer commits new code, the system should analyze the function signatures and existing code to generate initial unit test skeletons.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The system must identify the function parameters and return types.
     2. It should create a basic structure for unit tests (e.g., setup, teardown, test methods).
     3. Developers can easily modify these generated skeletons to add specific test logic.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a Developer, I want the system to automatically generate initial unit test skeletons based on the function signature and existing code logic..."

2. **FR-002: Comprehensive Unit Test Execution**
   - **Title:** The system runs a comprehensive suite of unit tests against new changes.
   - **Description:** Upon submission of a pull request (PR), the system should automatically run a full suite of unit tests to check for potential regressions.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The system must execute all relevant unit tests in the PR.
     2. Results must be displayed within the PR interface with clear pass/fail indicators.
     3. Developers receive immediate feedback on any failed tests.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a Developer, I want to submit a pull request (PR) and have the system run a comprehensive suite of unit tests against the new changes..."

3. **FR-003: Highlighting Poorly Covered Lines**
   - **Title:** The system highlights specific lines of code that are poorly covered by existing tests.
   - **Description:** The system should identify and highlight lines of code with low coverage, providing developers actionable insights.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The system must use branch coverage metrics to determine poorly covered lines.
     2. Developers can click on highlighted lines to view detailed coverage reports.
     3. The system provides a summary report indicating the percentage of code not covered by tests.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a Developer, I want the system to highlight specific lines of code that are poorly covered by existing tests (low coverage areas)..."

4. **FR-004: Custom Test Scenario Definition**
   - **Title:** The system allows QA Engineers to define custom test scenarios.
   - **Description:** QA Engineers can create complex and specific test scenarios that the LLM might overlook.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. QA Engineers can input detailed, business-specific test cases (e.g., "test failure when input is null AND user role is 'Guest'").
     2. The system should store these custom tests in a structured format for easy retrieval and execution.
     3. Custom tests take precedence over LLM-generated tests during execution.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a QA Engineer, I want to define custom, complex test scenarios (e.g., 'test failure when input is null AND user role is 'Guest''), so that I can ensure the system handles specific, high-risk business edge cases..."

5. **FR-005: Visual Dashboard for Test Coverage Trends**
   - **Title:** The system provides a visual dashboard showing test coverage trends over time.
   - **Description:** A dashboard should be available to track overall quality health of the codebase and justify refactoring efforts.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The dashboard must display historical coverage data (e.g., monthly, quarterly).
     2. It should include trend lines showing improvements or declines in coverage.
     3. Developers can drill down into specific time periods to view detailed reports.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a QA Engineer, I want the system to provide a visual dashboard showing test coverage trends over time..."

6. **FR-006: Manual Review and Modification of LLM-Generated Tests**
   - **Title:** The system allows manual review and modification of tests generated by the LLM.
   - **Description:** QA Engineers can manually review, approve, or modify any test generated by the LLM.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. QA Engineers have a UI to view and edit LLM-generated tests.
     2. The system should allow developers to mark tests as "approved" or "needs modification."
     3. Approved tests are stored in a database for future use.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a QA Engineer, I want the system to handle conflicts between custom test scenarios and LLM-generated tests..."

7. **FR-007: Rate Limits and Cost Tracking**
   - **Title:** The system configures rate limits and cost tracking for external LLM API calls.
   - **Description:** System Administrators can set up rate limits and track costs associated with LLM API usage.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The system should enforce predefined rate limits to prevent unexpected billing spikes.
     2. Cost tracking logs must be available for review by administrators.
     3. Alerts are sent when the rate limit is approaching or exceeded.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** Question 5: "What specific security measures will be in place to protect sensitive data during LLM API calls..."

### Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)

1. **NFR-001: Performance**
   - **Title:** The system must perform efficiently.
   - **Description:** The system should handle large codebases and multiple test runs without significant delays.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. Test execution times for a medium-sized project (5,000 lines of code) should not exceed 30 seconds.
     2. The dashboard updates in real-time with minimal latency.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a System Administrator, I want to configure rate limits and cost tracking for external LLM API calls..."

2. **NFR-002: Security**
   - **Title:** Sensitive data must be protected during LLM API calls.
   - **Description:** The system should implement robust security measures to protect sensitive data.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. PII (Personally Identifiable Information) is redacted before sending to the LLM API.
     2. Data in transit and at rest are encrypted using industry-standard protocols.
     3. Access controls ensure that only authorized personnel can use sensitive tools.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** Question 5: "What specific security measures will be in place to protect sensitive data during LLM API calls..."

3. **NFR-003: Usability**
   - **Title:** The system should be user-friendly and intuitive.
   - **Description:** The interface must be easy for developers, QA Engineers, and administrators to use.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. A clear and concise UI that allows users to easily navigate through the system.
     2. Intuitive controls for generating tests, reviewing test results, and managing custom scenarios.
     3. Comprehensive documentation and support resources are available.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a System Administrator, I want to manage and restrict access to specific testing tools and LLM APIs..."

4. **NFR-004: Scalability**
   - **Title:** The system should scale with the growing codebase.
   - **Description:** The system must handle increasing amounts of code without degradation in performance or functionality.
   - **Acceptance Criteria:**
     1. The system can handle a codebase of up to 50,000 lines of code efficiently.
     2. It should be able to scale horizontally by adding more resources as needed.
   - **Priority:** High
   - **Source:** User Story: "As a System Administrator, I want the system to provide a visual dashboard showing test coverage trends over time..."

### System Constraints and Assumptions

1. **Technical Constraints:**
   - The system must be compatible with major programming languages (Java, Python, JavaScript).
   - It should support integration with existing CI/CD pipelines.

2. **Business Constraints:**
   - The system must comply with industry-specific regulations (e.g., HIPAA for healthcare, GDPR for data privacy).

3. **Key Assumptions:**
   - Developers and QA Engineers will have access to the necessary tools and permissions.
   - The LLM API is reliable and available.

### Data Requirements

1. **Data Entities and Attributes:**
   - **Test Cases:** ID, Description, Code Snippet, Status (Pass/Fail), Execution Time
   - **Coverage Reports:** Line Number, Branch Coverage, Function Name, Test Case ID
   - **Custom Scenarios:** ID, Scenario Description, Input Parameters, Expected Output

2. **Data Validation Rules:**
   - Test cases must have valid descriptions and code snippets.
   - Custom scenarios must be well-formed and adhere to business rules.

3. **Data Relationships:**
   - Each test case is associated with a specific function or module.
   - Coverage reports link to the relevant lines of code and functions.
   - Custom scenarios are linked to specific developers or QA Engineers for review.

By providing these detailed functional, non-functional requirements, constraints, assumptions, and data entities, we ensure that all aspects of the system are thoroughly covered and meet the needs of various stakeholders.
entities:
### Actors (Users, External Systems, Stakeholders)

1. **Actor: Developer**
   - Name: Developer
   - Type: Actor
   - Description: A software developer responsible for writing code and maintaining the application.
   - Attributes: 
     - Role: Writes and commits new code, reviews generated test skeletons, modifies tests, submits pull requests (PRs).
   - Role: Developers use the system to generate initial unit test skeletons, run comprehensive tests, and review coverage reports.

2. **Actor: QA Engineer**
   - Name: QA Engineer
   - Type: Actor
   - Description: A quality assurance engineer responsible for defining custom test scenarios, reviewing generated tests, and ensuring high code coverage.
   - Attributes:
     - Role: Defines complex test scenarios, reviews LLM-generated tests, approves or modifies tests, generates detailed coverage reports.
   - Role: QA Engineers use the system to define custom test scenarios, review generated tests, and ensure comprehensive testing.

3. **Actor: System Administrator**
   - Name: System Administrator
   - Type: Actor
   - Description: A system administrator responsible for managing the system's configuration, security settings, and performance.
   - Attributes:
     - Role: Configures rate limits, tracks costs, sets up access controls, manages dashboards.
   - Role: Administrators use the system to configure rate limits, track costs, manage access controls, and monitor test coverage trends.

### Data Objects (Entities, Files, Databases, Information)

1. **Data Object: Test Case**
   - Name: Test Case
   - Type: Data Object
   - Description: A unit test that is generated or defined by the system.
   - Attributes:
     - ID: Unique identifier for each test case.
     - Description: Detailed description of the test scenario.
     - Code Snippet: The actual code snippet used in the test.
     - Status (Pass/Fail): Indicates whether the test passed or failed.
     - Execution Time: Time taken to execute the test.
   - Constraints:
     - Test cases must have valid descriptions and code snippets.

2. **Data Object: Coverage Report**
   - Name: Coverage Report
   - Type: Data Object
   - Description: A report detailing line-by-line coverage of the codebase.
   - Attributes:
     - Line Number: The specific line number in the code.
     - Branch Coverage: Percentage of branches covered by tests.
     - Function Name: The name of the function associated with the line.
     - Test Case ID: Reference to the test case that covers this line.
   - Constraints:
     - Coverage reports must be generated based on branch coverage metrics.

3. **Data Object: Custom Scenario**
   - Name: Custom Scenario
   - Type: Data Object
   - Description: A custom, business-specific test scenario defined by QA Engineers.
   - Attributes:
     - ID: Unique identifier for each custom scenario.
     - Scenario Description: Detailed description of the custom test case.
     - Input Parameters: The input parameters required to execute the test.
     - Expected Output: The expected output from executing the test.
   - Constraints:
     - Custom scenarios must be well-formed and adhere to business rules.

### Processes (Functions, Services, Workflows, Operations)

1. **Process: Generate Test Skeletons**
   - Name: Generate Test Skeletons
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that automatically generates initial unit test skeletons based on function signatures.
   - Inputs:
     - Function Signatures: The input parameters and return types of the functions to be tested.
     - Existing Code Logic: The existing codebase used for generating test skeletons.
   - Outputs:
     - Initial Unit Test Skeletons: Basic structure for unit tests, including setup, teardown, and test methods.
   - Triggers:
     - Developer Commits New Code

2. **Process: Run Comprehensive Tests**
   - Name: Run Comprehensive Tests
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that runs a full suite of unit tests against new changes submitted via pull requests (PRs).
   - Inputs:
     - Pull Request (PR): The code changes to be tested.
     - Unit Test Suite: The set of predefined and custom test cases.
   - Outputs:
     - Test Results: Pass/fail indicators for each test case.
   - Triggers:
     - PR Submission

3. **Process: Highlight Poorly Covered Lines**
   - Name: Highlight Poorly Covered Lines
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that identifies and highlights lines of code with low coverage, providing actionable insights to developers.
   - Inputs:
     - Codebase: The current state of the application's source code.
     - Coverage Metrics: Branch coverage data for the codebase.
   - Outputs:
     - Highlighted Lines: Specific lines of code identified as poorly covered.
     - Detailed Coverage Reports: Reports indicating low coverage areas and percentage of uncovered code.
   - Triggers:
     - Code Commit or Change

4. **Process: Define Custom Test Scenarios**
   - Name: Define Custom Test Scenarios
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that allows QA Engineers to create complex, business-specific test scenarios.
   - Inputs:
     - Business Requirements: The specific requirements and edge cases defined by the business.
     - Custom Scenario Descriptions: Detailed descriptions of the custom tests.
   - Outputs:
     - Custom Test Scenarios: Structured format for storing and executing custom tests.
   - Triggers:
     - QA Engineer Input

5. **Process: Visual Dashboard for Test Coverage Trends**
   - Name: Visual Dashboard for Test Coverage Trends
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that provides a visual dashboard to track overall quality health of the codebase over time.
   - Inputs:
     - Historical Coverage Data: Past coverage metrics and trends.
     - Real-Time Updates: Current state of test coverage.
   - Outputs:
     - Dashboard Display: Real-time updates with trend lines showing improvements or declines in coverage.
     - Detailed Reports: Drill-down capabilities to view specific time periods.
   - Triggers:
     - Periodic Updates (e.g., monthly, quarterly)

6. **Process: Manual Review and Modification of LLM-Generated Tests**
   - Name: Manual Review and Modification
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that allows QA Engineers to review, approve, or modify tests generated by the LLM.
   - Inputs:
     - Generated Test Cases: Tests created by the LLM.
     - Custom Scenarios: Predefined custom test scenarios.
   - Outputs:
     - Approved Tests: Tests marked as approved for future use.
     - Modified Tests: Tests that have been modified by QA Engineers.
   - Triggers:
     - LLM Generation

7. **Process: Rate Limits and Cost Tracking**
   - Name: Rate Limits and Cost Tracking
   - Type: Process
   - Description: A process that configures rate limits and tracks costs associated with external LLM API usage.
   - Inputs:
     - Rate Limit Settings: Configuration settings for rate limits.
     - Cost Data: Pricing information for LLM API usage.
   - Outputs:
     - Rate Limits: Configured limits to prevent excessive API calls.
     - Cost Tracking: Detailed records of API usage and associated costs.
   - Triggers:
     - System Initialization or Change

### System Components (Modules, Services, Interfaces)

1. **System Component: Test Generation Module**
   - Name: Test Generation Module
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A module responsible for generating initial unit test skeletons based on function signatures.
   - Responsibilities:
     - Generate test skeletons from code commits.
     - Integrate with the CI/CD pipeline to ensure continuous testing.
   - Interfaces:
     - Integration with Code Repository (e.g., Git).
     - Communication with Test Execution Service.

2. **System Component: Test Execution Service**
   - Name: Test Execution Service
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A service responsible for executing comprehensive tests against pull requests.
   - Responsibilities:
     - Run predefined and custom test cases.
     - Provide pass/fail indicators for each test case.
   - Interfaces:
     - Integration with CI/CD Pipeline.
     - Communication with Test Reporting Service.

3. **System Component: Coverage Analysis Module**
   - Name: Coverage Analysis Module
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A module responsible for analyzing code coverage and generating detailed reports.
   - Responsibilities:
     - Analyze branch coverage metrics.
     - Highlight lines of code with low coverage.
     - Generate detailed coverage reports.
   - Interfaces:
     - Integration with Code Repository.
     - Communication with Test Reporting Service.

4. **System Component: Custom Scenario Management Module**
   - Name: Custom Scenario Management Module
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A module responsible for managing custom test scenarios defined by QA Engineers.
   - Responsibilities:
     - Store and manage custom scenario definitions.
     - Integrate with the Test Execution Service to execute custom tests.
   - Interfaces:
     - Integration with User Interface (UI) for defining custom scenarios.
     - Communication with Test Execution Service.

5. **System Component: Dashboard Service**
   - Name: Dashboard Service
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A service responsible for providing a visual dashboard to track test coverage trends over time.
   - Responsibilities:
     - Display real-time updates and trend lines.
     - Provide drill-down capabilities for detailed reports.
   - Interfaces:
     - Integration with Data Storage (e.g., database).
     - Communication with Test Reporting Service.

6. **System Component: Rate Limiting and Cost Tracking Module**
   - Name: Rate Limiting and Cost Tracking Module
   - Type: System Component
   - Description: A module responsible for configuring rate limits and tracking costs associated with external LLM API usage.
relationships:
### 1. ASSOCIATION RELATIONSHIPS (uses, interacts with, accesses)

1. **Source Entity:** Test Case
   - **Target Entity:** Function
   - **Relationship Type:** Association
   - **Relationship Name:** "uses"
   - **Cardinality:** 1:many
   - **Description:** A test case uses one or more functions to validate its behavior.

2. **Source Entity:** Custom Scenario
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Association
   - **Relationship Name:** "includes"
   - **Cardinality:** many:1
   - **Description:** A custom scenario includes a specific test case that it defines or modifies.

3. **Source Entity:** Coverage Report
   - **Target Entity:** Line of Code
   - **Relationship Type:** Association
   - **Relationship Name:** "accesses"
   - **Cardinality:** 1:many
   - **Description:** A coverage report accesses one or more lines of code to determine branch coverage.

4. **Source Entity:** Test Execution Log
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Association
   - **Relationship Name:** "records"
   - **Cardinality:** 1:many
   - **Description:** A test execution log records the results of one or more test cases.

5. **Source Entity:** Dashboard
   - **Target Entity:** Coverage Report
   - **Relationship Type:** Association
   - **Relationship Name:** "displays"
   - **Cardinality:** 1:many
   - **Description:** A dashboard displays multiple coverage reports to show overall test coverage trends.

### 2. COMPOSITION RELATIONSHIPS (contains, consists of, includes)

1. **Source Entity:** Test Suite
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Composition
   - **Relationship Name:** "consists of"
   - **Cardinality:** many:many
   - **Description:** A test suite consists of multiple test cases that are part of the suite.

2. **Source Entity:** Custom Scenario Suite
   - **Target Entity:** Custom Scenario
   - **Relationship Type:** Composition
   - **Relationship Name:** "consists of"
   - **Cardinality:** many:many
   - **Description:** A custom scenario suite consists of multiple custom scenarios that are part of the suite.

3. **Source Entity:** Coverage Report
   - **Target Entity:** Line of Code
   - **Relationship Type:** Composition
   - **Relationship Name:** "consists of"
   - **Cardinality:** many:many
   - **Description:** A coverage report consists of multiple lines of code that are analyzed for branch coverage.

### 3. DEPENDENCY RELATIONSHIPS (depends on, requires, needs)

1. **Source Entity:** Test Case
   - **Target Entity:** Function
   - **Relationship Type:** Dependency
   - **Relationship Name:** "depends on"
   - **Strength:** strong
   - **Description:** A test case depends on the correct functionality of a specific function to validate its behavior.

2. **Source Entity:** Custom Scenario
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Dependency
   - **Relationship Name:** "depends on"
   - **Strength:** strong
   - **Description:** A custom scenario depends on the existence and correctness of a specific test case to define its behavior.

3. **Source Entity:** Coverage Report
   - **Target Entity:** Line of Code
   - **Relationship Type:** Dependency
   - **Relationship Name:** "depends on"
   - **Strength:** strong
   - **Description:** A coverage report depends on the lines of code being executed to determine branch coverage.

4. **Source Entity:** Test Execution Log
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Dependency
   - **Relationship Name:** "depends on"
   - **Strength:** strong
   - **Description:** A test execution log depends on the results of specific test cases to record their outcomes.

### 4. INHERITANCE RELATIONSHIPS (is a type of, specializes)

1. **Source Entity:** Test Case
   - **Target Entity:** Test Suite
   - **Relationship Type:** Inheritance
   - **Relationship Name:** "is a type of"
   - **Description:** A test case is a specific instance or member of a test suite.

2. **Source Entity:** Custom Scenario
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case
   - **Relationship Type:** Inheritance
   - **Relationship Name:** "is a type of"
   - **Description:** A custom scenario is a specialized form of a test case that includes additional context or constraints.

### 5. FLOW RELATIONSHIPS (data flow, control flow, communication)

1. **Source Entity:** LLM API Call
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case Code
   - **Relationship Type:** Flow
   - **Flow Type:** data
   - **Flow Content:** Input parameters and test case code snippets
   - **Direction:** unidirectional
   - **Description:** Data flows from the LLM API call to the test case code, providing input for generating or executing tests.

2. **Source Entity:** Test Execution Log
   - **Target Entity:** Dashboard
   - **Relationship Type:** Flow
   - **Flow Type:** data
   - **Flow Content:** Test results and coverage reports
   - **Direction:** unidirectional
   - **Description:** Data flows from the test execution log to the dashboard, updating it with new test results and coverage information.

3. **Source Entity:** User Interface (UI)
   - **Target Entity:** Test Case Editor
   - **Relationship Type:** Flow
   - **Flow Type:** control
   - **Flow Content:** User commands and actions
   - **Direction:** unidirectional
   - **Description:** Control flows from the user interface to the test case editor, allowing users to create or modify test cases.

4. **Source Entity:** Test Case Editor
   - **Target Entity:** LLM API Call
   - **Relationship Type:** Flow
   - **Flow Type:** control
   - **Flow Content:** User commands and actions
   - **Direction:** unidirectional
   - **Description:** Control flows from the test case editor to the LLM API call, sending user-defined parameters for generating or modifying tests.

These relationships ensure that all entities are logically connected and function cohesively within the software testing system.
check_results:
Based on the provided user stories, I will conduct a comprehensive analysis of the software testing system's requirements.

**COMPLETENESS ANALYSIS**

1. **Missing functional requirements**: The system does not have explicit requirements for handling test data management, such as storing and retrieving test data, or supporting multiple test environments.
2. **Missing non-functional requirements**: There are no specific requirements for security, scalability, or performance metrics.
3. **Uncovered user scenarios**: The system does not account for the scenario where a developer wants to run a subset of tests on a specific code change.
4. **Missing edge cases and error handling**: The system lacks explicit requirements for handling test failures, such as providing detailed logs or notifications.
5. Score: 8/10

**CONSISTENCY ANALYSIS**

1. **Internal conflicts among requirements**: There are conflicting assumptions about the role of the LLM in generating tests (e.g., "LLM is a tool" vs. "LLM generates tests").
2. **Terminology inconsistencies**: The term "test suite" is used interchangeably with "test set," which may lead to confusion.
3. **Conflicting assumptions or constraints**: There are conflicting requirements about the level of human involvement in testing (e.g., "LLM generates tests" vs. "QA engineer reviews and approves tests").
4. Misaligned entities and relationships: The system does not clearly define the relationship between test cases, test suites, and code changes.
5. Score: 7/10

**CLARITY ANALYSIS**

1. **Ambiguous requirements**: Some requirements are unclear or open-ended (e.g., "make this test more robust").
2. **Unclear acceptance criteria**: The system lacks explicit acceptance criteria for testing (e.g., what constitutes a successful test run).
3. **Vague or imprecise language**: Requirements use vague terms like "highly specific" and "domain-specific."
4. Missing definitions: Key concepts, such as "test complexity" and "test scope," are not defined.
5. Score: 6/10

**CORRECTNESS ANALYSIS**

1. **Requirements that do not match user stories**: Some requirements seem to contradict the user stories (e.g., "LLM generates tests" vs. "QA engineer reviews and approves tests").
2. **Technically unfeasible specifications**: The system requires the LLM to generate tests for legacy codebases, which may be technically challenging.
3. **Incorrect business rules**: There are conflicting assumptions about the role of testing in the development process (e.g., "testing is a gate" vs. "testing is an iterative process").
4. Missing or wrong dependencies: The system does not account for the dependency between test results and code changes.
5. Score: 8/10

**TESTABILITY ANALYSIS**

1. **Requirements that cannot be tested**: Some requirements are too vague or open-ended to be tested (e.g., "make this test more robust").
2. **Missing measurable criteria**: The system lacks explicit measurable criteria for testing success (e.g., what constitutes a successful test run).
3. Unclear success conditions: Requirements do not clearly define the success conditions for testing.
4. Verification challenges: The system does not account for the challenge of verifying complex or edge-case scenarios.
5. Score: 7/10

**TRACEABILITY ANALYSIS**

1. **Requirements not traceable to user stories**: Some requirements seem to be disconnected from specific user stories (e.g., "LLM generates tests" vs. "QA engineer reviews and approves tests").
2. Missing requirement relationships: The system does not clearly define the relationship between test cases, test suites, and code changes.
3. Orphaned or duplicated requirements: There are duplicate or orphaned requirements that do not contribute to the overall system functionality.
4. Gaps in impact analysis: The system lacks explicit consideration of the impact on testing speed, accuracy, and efficiency.
5. Score: 8/10

**OVERALL ASSESSMENT**

- Overall Quality Score: 7.2/10
- Ready for Implementation: No
- Critical Issues Count: 4
- Major Issues Count: 6
- Minor Issues Count: 3
srs_document:
Based on the provided requirements, I will create a complete SRS document with the specified structure.

**Software Requirements Specification**
## Test Integration System

### Document Information
- **Document Title:** Software Requirements Specification for Test Integration System
- **Version:** 1.0
- **Date:** [Current Date]
- **Domain:** Software Testing
- **Status:** Draft/Final

---

## Table of Contents
1. Introduction
2. Overall Description
3. System Features
4. External Interface Requirements
5. Non-Functional Requirements
6. System Models
7. Verification and Validation
8. Appendices

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose
The purpose of this Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document is to provide a comprehensive description of the Test Integration System, including its functional and non-functional requirements.

### 1.2 Scope
This SRS document covers the development of the Test Integration System, which will integrate various testing tools and frameworks to automate testing processes for software applications.

### 1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations

- **Test Integration System (TIS):** A system that integrates multiple testing tools and frameworks to automate testing processes.
- **Testing Tools:** Software applications used for testing purposes, such as unit testing, integration testing, and UI testing.
- **Frameworks:** Programming libraries or architectures used for building software applications.

### 1.4 References

- [1] IEEE Standard for Software Requirements Specifications (IEEE Std 830-1998)
- [2] ISO/IEC 9126:2003 - Software Engineering - Product Quality

### 1.5 Overview
This SRS document provides a detailed description of the Test Integration System, including its functional and non-functional requirements.

## 2. Overall Description

### 2.1 Product Perspective
The Test Integration System will be used by software developers to automate testing processes for their applications. It will integrate various testing tools and frameworks to provide a comprehensive testing solution.

### 2.2 Product Functions
- **Test Case Management:** The system will manage test cases, including creating, editing, and executing them.
- **Test Data Management:** The system will manage test data, including generating, storing, and retrieving it.
- **Test Execution:** The system will execute tests, including running automated tests and reporting results.

### 2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- **Software Developers:** Users who will use the Test Integration System to automate testing processes for their applications.
- **Quality Assurance (QA) Engineers:** Users who will use the Test Integration System to manage test cases and test data.

### 2.4 Operating Environment
The system will run on a Windows or Linux operating system, with a minimum of 8 GB RAM and 1 TB storage.

### 2.5 Design and Implementation Constraints

- **Scalability:** The system must be able to handle large amounts of testing data.
- **Security:** The system must ensure the security and integrity of test data.

### 2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies
The development of the Test Integration System assumes that the necessary testing tools and frameworks will be available for integration.

## 3. System Features

### 3.1 Functional Requirements

#### FR-001: Test Case Management
- **Title:** Manage test cases.
- **Description:** The system must allow users to create, edit, and execute test cases.
- **Priority:** High
- **Source:** User Story 1
- **Acceptance Criteria:**

*   The system allows users to create new test cases.
*   The system allows users to edit existing test cases.
*   The system executes test cases.

#### FR-002: Test Data Management
- **Title:** Manage test data.
- **Description:** The system must allow users to generate, store, and retrieve test data.
- **Priority:** Medium
- **Source:** User Story 2
- **Acceptance Criteria:**

*   The system generates test data based on user input.
*   The system stores test data in a secure database.
*   The system retrieves test data for execution.

#### FR-003: Test Execution
- **Title:** Execute tests.
- **Description:** The system must execute tests, including running automated tests and reporting results.
- **Priority:** High
- **Source:** User Story 3
- **Acceptance Criteria:**

*   The system runs automated tests based on user input.
*   The system reports test results to users.

### 3.2 Business Rules

- **Rule 1:** Test cases must be executed in the correct order.
- **Rule 2:** Test data must be generated and stored securely.

## 4. External Interface Requirements

### 4.1 User Interfaces
The system will have a user-friendly interface for users to interact with it.

### 4.2 Hardware Interfaces
The system will run on Windows or Linux operating systems, with a minimum of 8 GB RAM and 1 TB storage.

### 4.3 Software Interfaces
The system will integrate with various testing tools and frameworks.

### 4.4 Communications Interfaces
The system will communicate with users through the user interface.

## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

### 5.1 Performance Requirements
The system must be able to handle large amounts of testing data.

### 5.2 Security Requirements
The system must ensure the security and integrity of test data.

### 5.3 Reliability Requirements
The system must be reliable and available for users.

### 5.4 Usability Requirements
The system must have a user-friendly interface for users to interact with it.

### 5.5 Maintainability Requirements
The system must be maintainable by developers.

### 5.6 Portability Requirements
The system must be portable across different operating systems.

## 6. System Models

### 6.1 Data Model
The data model will include test cases, test data, and user information.

### 6.2 Process Model
The process model will include the creation, execution, and reporting of tests.

### 6.3 System Architecture
The system architecture will include a front-end interface for users to interact with it, a back-end database for storing test data, and an integration layer for integrating with testing tools and frameworks.

## 7. Verification and Validation

### 7.1 Verification Methods
- **Unit Testing:** The system will be unit tested to ensure that individual components function correctly.
- **Integration Testing:** The system will be integrated tested to ensure that components work together correctly.
- **System Testing:** The system will be system tested to ensure that it meets user requirements.

### 7.2 Validation Criteria
The system will meet the following validation criteria:

*   It must allow users to create, edit, and execute test cases.
*   It must generate, store, and retrieve test data securely.
*   It must run automated tests based on user input and report results.

### 7.3 Testing Requirements
- **Test Cases:** The system will be tested with a set of predefined test cases.
- **Test Data:** The system will be tested with different types of test data.
- **User Interface:** The system will be tested for usability and user experience.

## 8. Appendices

### Appendix A: Traceability Matrix
The traceability matrix will show the relationship between requirements and user stories.

### Appendix B: Glossary
The glossary will define key terms used in this document.

### Appendix C: Quality Assessment
The quality assessment will provide a summary of the quality check results.

---

**Document Control:**
- Created by: MARE Requirements Engineering Framework
- Review Status: [Pending/Approved]
- Next Review Date: [Date]

This SRS document provides a comprehensive description of the Test Integration System, including its functional and non-functional requirements. It ensures that all stakeholders have a clear understanding of the system's capabilities and limitations.

Please let me know if you need any further assistance or modifications to this document.
